summary: Isaiah warns against trusting Egypt's horses instead of the LORD, promises a righteous king who shelters his people, pronounces the destroyer's own destruction, and declares sweeping judgment on Edom, while Psalm 76 celebrates God's decisive victory at Zion over an army disarmed by his rebuke alone.
psalm_rows_html:
PsalmTypeAuthorSituationKey ThemeNotable VerseNT Connection
76Praise / Song of Zion (Victory Psalm)AsaphComposed to celebrate a dramatic deliverance from a besieging army, a defeat accomplished by God's rebuke alone rather than by battle — traditionally associated with the destruction of Sennacherib's Assyrian forces outside Jerusalem, fitting well alongside this same day's reading in Isaiah 31-34God's awesome, unassailable power to defend Zion and disarm hostile armies without a sword being drawn by human hands"Surely the wrath of man shall praise you; the remnant of wrath you will put on like a belt" (Psalm 76:10)God's sovereign judgment over hostile powers at Zion anticipates Christ's triumph over the rulers and authorities, disarming them and putting them to open shame by the cross (Colossians 2:15)
